metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the full question text for `respects_artifact_altitude` to the plan body (mirroring the inline text given for `committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim`), so the `_BINARY_DESC` entry and verifier prompt bullet have a concrete, plan-anchored string to copy.
findings.finding: The question text (value vocabulary) for `respects_artifact_altitude` is never defined in the plan. The plan names the key, states its decision-level semantics ('graded VALIDITY input (low score DROPS the FP)'), but never supplies the actual description string for `_BINARY_DESC["respects_artifact_altitude"]` or the bullet in the verifier prompts. An implementer has no text to write.
findings.location: ## What (verified code targets) — 'respects_artifact_altitude (FP-1)' paragraph; AC line 1 (Both keys added to … _BINARY_DESC … both verifier prompts)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence like: 'respects_artifact_altitude question text: "yes|no|insufficient|na — the finding targets the appropriate level of artifact (plan/AC/task) for the claimed defect; na if the criterion is level-agnostic."' adjacent to the existing FP-1 description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the ADR AC, e.g., `grep -l 'graded.*field\|prompt.*gloss' docs/adr/*.md` (or equivalent), so the criterion is deterministically evaluable in-session.
findings.finding: The ADR acceptance criterion has no concrete proving command; 'recorded in a new ADR under docs/adr/' states an action but provides no deterministic command (e.g., ls or grep) that produces evidence the file exists with the required rationale content.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Rationale for graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss recorded in a new ADR under docs/adr/'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the ADR AC: 'Proof: `grep -rl "graded-field" docs/adr/` returns the new file AND the file contains a section titled "Decision" with content referencing both graded-field and prompt-gloss trade-offs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ADR-authoring step in the 'What' section (or a dedicated Deliverables subsection) that names the target file, the decision being recorded (graded-field vs. prompt-gloss), and the options considered, so AC#4 is covered by described work.
findings.finding: AC#4 requires authoring an ADR under docs/adr/ (with a named location), but the 'What' section makes no mention of this deliverable at all — the ADR is an orphan criterion covering undescribed work.
findings.suggested_fix: Under 'What', add a bullet: '(d) Author docs/adr/NNNN-graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss.md recording the design decision that commits warrant a graded binary field while clarifications warrant a prompt gloss, with the rejected alternative (veto flag) noted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the comment on `test_review_kernel_rules.py` line 74 from '7 graded sub-questions' to '9 graded sub-questions' (or make it dynamic, e.g. `f'{len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY)} graded sub-questions'`) as part of this story's changes.
findings.finding: `test_atomicity_each_binary_subquestion_is_an_independent_field` in `tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py` (line 77) asserts `len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1`, hardcoding the exact field count. Adding 2 new keys to `GRADED_BINARY` will cause this test to remain consistent (both sides of the equality grow together), but a secondary assertion `assert len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1` does hold — wait, it checks `len(fields) == len(GRADED_BINARY) + 1`. If GRADED_BINARY grows by 2, `fields` (derived from the model, which is also derived from GRADED_BINARY) also grows by 2. So `len(fields)` == `len(GRADED_BINARY) + 1` still holds. No breakage. However, the comment on that line says '7 graded sub-questions' — the hardcoded comment will become stale (9 after the change). The plan does not mention updating this comment/test annotation.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py line 74 (comment: '# the 7 graded sub-questions are each their own field, plus the conditional veto')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the hardcoded comment with: `# the graded sub-questions are each their own field, plus the conditional veto`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `test_verifier_new_subanswers` and the `committed_claim` calibration tests belong in `test_review_kernel_rules.py` / `test_review_kernel.py` (the existing homes for kernel behavioral assertions) rather than `test_plan_review_evals.py`, and update the AC proof locations accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan names `tests/unit/test_plan_review_evals.py` as the home for both `test_verifier_new_subanswers` and the calibration tests (`-k committed_claim`). Existing tests in that file exclusively test eval-spec structure (prompt file parsing, dataset shape, scorer presence) — they do NOT exercise kernel math or `GRADED_BINARY` directly. Placing behavioral/arithmetic kernel tests there is inconsistent with that file's established role, and those tests would not naturally fit alongside `test_eval_spec_validates` et al.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — both '`pytest -k verifier_new_subanswers`' and '`pytest -k committed_claim`' point to `tests/unit/test_plan_review_evals.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the ADR requirement to the 'What' section (or title) so the full scope is declared upfront and an implementer reading the plan top-to-bottom encounters no undeclared deliverable.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria introduce an ADR deliverable ('Rationale for graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss recorded in a new ADR under docs/adr/') that is not mentioned in the title ('Pass-2 vocabulary expansion') or in the 'What' section, representing undeclared scope beyond the stated intent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Rationale for graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss recorded in a new ADR...'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, append a bullet: 'Write ADR under docs/adr/ capturing the rationale for using a graded GRADED_BINARY field vs. a prompt gloss for these two criteria.' This makes the ADR visible before the ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration/eval AC that exercises the updated verifier prompts against a live (or recorded-replay) LLM response, or explicitly document why this is deferred (e.g., a named follow-on story) and confirm the unit tests use fixture responses that faithfully reproduce the new field structure.
findings.finding: The calibration-test AC (AC 5) places proving tests in `tests/unit/`, which conventionally mocks LLM calls; the verifier prompt changes (plan_review_verifier.md and _agentic.md) are live-model-facing additions that would be bypassed by mocked tests, leaving no E2E criterion that exercises the new sub-answer fields against a real LLM response.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Calibration: RECALL case … AND a PRECISION case … Proof: author both in tests/unit/test_plan_review_evals.py; pytest -k committed_claim green'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback statement to the 'What' section (e.g., 'if the replay test fails, the change is reverted and the na-default contract is redesigned before re-attempting') so the reversibility mechanism is unambiguous, satisfying T4's requirement beyond mere acknowledgement.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that validity() changes affect old sidecars and mandates a unit-test guard, but provides no explicit rollback/back-out path if the na-default implementation is incorrect — acknowledgement + a test alone does not satisfy T4's reversibility requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC step (or extend the existing AC-1 proof) to update `test_atomicity_each_binary_subquestion_is_an_independent_field` in `tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py` to account for the two new GRADED_BINARY keys (the hard-coded `len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1` assertion stays correct — it need only be re-derived from the tuple, not a literal — confirming the count-check survives vocabulary growth).
findings.finding: Adding two keys to `GRADED_BINARY` will immediately break the existing `test_atomicity_each_binary_subquestion_is_an_independent_field` test, which hard-codes `len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1` (currently 7+1=8). The plan does not mention updating this test, so `pytest -k verifier_new_subanswers` green will coexist with the atomicity test going red — a mechanism failure hiding behind the AC's narrow test scope.
findings.location: tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py:77 / AC: 'Both keys added to decide.GRADED_BINARY + _BINARY_DESC + both verifier prompts'
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_review_kernel_rules.py:test_atomicity_each_binary_subquestion_is_an_independent_field`, the count assertion is already expressed as `len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1` (not a literal integer), so it will actually continue to pass automatically as long as `_binary_fields()` is derived from the same tuple. Verify this is the case by re-reading line 77: the assertion is dynamic, not a fixed magic number — if so, this is a non-issue and the plan is clean on this point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the exact prose of the FP-2 `evidence_entails_finding` restatement gloss to the plan (e.g., the sentence to insert verbatim into both verifier prompts), so AC#3's grep proof tests a meaningful, plan-anchored phrase rather than an arbitrary implementation detail.
findings.finding: The `evidence_entails_finding` restatement gloss (FP-2) text is never specified in the plan. The plan mandates the gloss be added to both verifier prompts and gives a grep anchor ('already states'), but never defines the actual wording to insert. The existing prompts contain no such gloss (confirmed by reading both files).
findings.location: ## What — 'FP-2 gloss on evidence_entails_finding' clause; AC line 3 (evidence_entails_finding restatement gloss … grep -l 'already states' …)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly add 'update `test_atomicity_each_binary_subquestion_is_an_independent_field` to reflect the expanded GRADED_BINARY count' to the plan's What section or AC#1 proof steps, so the structural-guard test remains a valid co-located schema contract.
findings.finding: `test_atomicity_each_binary_subquestion_is_an_independent_field` in `tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py` (line 77) asserts `len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1`, hard-coding the current field count. Adding 2 keys to GRADED_BINARY will immediately break this test. The plan does not identify updating this structural test as a required step.
findings.location: AC line 1 (Both keys added to decide.GRADED_BINARY + _BINARY_DESC + both verifier prompts); tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py line 77
findings.suggested_fix: Update the comment in test_review_kernel_rules.py line 74 from '7 graded' to '9 graded' and keep the `len(fields) == len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY) + 1` assertion as-is (it remains correct by construction since it derives from GRADED_BINARY dynamically).
